days.headline: Rolling Ariège roads to Foix the day after a summit finish — classic breakaway territory.

picks.why: Wildcard-team riders have to hunt stages like this one.
Climbs well enough to survive the Pyrenean foothills and often rides ahead of the GC group.
Cheap engine for a big escape day — EF always races aggressively.

days.headline: Flat run into Pau — the sprinters' first clear chance, so their teams will control it.

picks.why: Arguably the fastest man in the race on a straightforward finish.
Best leadout in the peloton and scores consistently even when he doesn't win.
Podium-regular sprinter who also survives harder finishes — great points-per-euro.

days.headline: The queen Pyrenean stage to Gavarnie-Gèdre — 4,149 m of climbing, biggest points day of the window.

picks.why: Long, high mountain days are where he's historically strongest, and the TTT showed Visma arrived sharp.
The default pick for any big summit day — he'll be hunting bonus seconds and stage points.
Likely to hunt the breakaway and mountain points if he's out of the GC picture.

days.headline: Pan-flat run to Bordeaux (only 701 m of climbing all day) — a nailed-on bunch sprint to close the window.

picks.why: The most conventional sprint finish of the week suits him perfectly.
Bordeaux's long finishing straights reward a big leadout train.
Reliable top-5 sprinter who keeps scoring on days the pure flyers fade.

days.headline: Périgueux to Bergerac is gently rolling (1,353 m) — sprint day, but a late escape isn't impossible.

picks.why: Back-to-back sprint chances reward keeping him through the weekend.
Strong enough to score even if the run-in turns scrappy.
Consistent top-5 threat and cheaper than the two marquee sprinters.

days.headline: Malemort to Ussel packs 3,244 m of Massif Central chop — a puncheur or breakaway day, not a sprint.

picks.why: Punchy terrain like this is exactly his profile and he started the race in sharp form.
French puncheur who thrives on repeated short climbs and hunts breakaways on home roads.
Relentless attacker — days with constant up-and-down are where he scores biggest.

fantasy.strategy: The stage 1-7 transfer window closes after Bordeaux, so spend what's left of its 8 transfers making sure two sprinters are aboard for stages 5 and 7 while keeping both marquee GC riders through the Pyrenees — stage 6 to Gavarnie-Gèdre is the biggest single pot of the window and the best place to play the double-points bonus if you still have it. When the stage 8-14 window opens you get 8 fresh transfers: hold the sprinters one more day for Bergerac, then rotate one out for a puncheur or breakaway rider ahead of the choppy Ussel stage 9. Keep the budget balanced toward climbers overall — climbing stages outscore sprints, and the race only gets hillier from here.
